item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is of Robert Mapplethorpe's White Gauze, which is copyrighted by the Robert Mapplethorpe Foundation, making it non-compliant with the wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of different ways that a surgery could "fail", and The Foundation would make note of the details here because it sheds light on the mechanics of the anomaly.

-Does the cutting work, but the patients die for no clear medical reason?
-Do the patients die under the knife do to seemingly mundane but always unlikely medical complications?
-Do random coincidences keep happening in defiance of statistical common sense that keep the surgery from being performed?
-Does a scalpel just not cut the joining? Despite skin being fully vulnerable outside of attempts at separation?

See how each of those has radically different implications for what Xing is and does?

From the interview; two weeks from calling a lawyer to a divorce being final is insanely fast. Even under ideal conditions where the parties agree immediately and don't have anything to fight about, you're looking at a month or two just waiting for the court to process the paperwork1.

Footnotes
1. Based on personal experience and advice from more than one divorce lawyer.

item.title: Interesting ideas for this post.
item.content:encoded: You should go into detail of what happens when you attempt to seperate a “Xing Couple.” Or cover other couples and their experiences with Xing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to know how -1A and -1B are fused. Neutral vote until then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just out of morbid curiosity, what did you think it was going to be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, seems like you're right. There's one instance where the foundation itself refers to the SCP as "he", describing how the SCP-1362-1 can't remember anything other than his appearance, but even then it's probably reporting SCP-1362-1's words, so it's all good. Thanks for the response. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as I can tell, it's only the SCP-1632-1 instance that refers to SCP-1632 as "he." That would make sense since they're not part of the SCP Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this SCP, its creepy but cool, I never imagined a divorce lawyer being a sort of horror story, the couple being fused together is incredibly creepy but it actually fits in quite well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My bet's on nothing. If Xing's just trying to make people happy again, it's probably worth noting that while divorces are hard on kids, two unhappy parents staying together for whatever reason are even worse. Happy kids require parents that like each other. This might be hella weird, but it's probably better psychologically for the kiddos.

As for when the foundation gets the parents, kiddos are probably amnesticized and put with next-of-kin/the foster system, since I'm assuming both parents are considered dead as per the cover story.

item.title: extention here and there perhaps?
item.content:encoded:
Just some things I want to know about really, like how the surgery fails. Perhaps some expansion on how the Foundation has attempted to contain 1632? I feel like 'attempts to get the number' stuff is a little short, and doesn't really make sense. Why don't they just ask one of the divorcees to give them the number? Or why don't they put a tracking device on a divorcee? Surely there are D-class who want a divorce. I dunno. Maybe I'm just dumb.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May be in the dialouge you can make the couple relunctant to explain that there relationship was bad but finally admit that despite their experience there much happier than they are before most couples don't like explaining or admiting there personal issues out loud who knows that could be the reason why they didn't get along just a pitch is all
and sorry about the bad grammar I just don't see effort in correcting a comment *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice one. I'll give it a +1 as soon as I'm done typing this. I fixed some of the grammar issues, hopefully it's okay. Also, the article sometimes uses "it" when talking about the SCP, while it uses "he" in other parts. I didn't edit this one because I felt guilty changing entire words, but I just wanted to let you know. As for the content itself, it's a creepy and interesting one. The dialogue is a little unrealistic if you ask me, both because of the way they talk and the fact that they never interrupt each other and know exactly when it's their turn to talk. One last thing: How does the foundation know that the ads have symbols on them? Aren't they supposed to appear as numbers to whoever sees them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took the clunky dialog that they've been altered in some way. I promise you, love or not you would have to royally fuck with my brain to have me be cool with being physically fused to another person.
Neat, concise, freaky, with a little twist. I like!
If you know what I mean. ( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird, and creepy, and weirdly, creepily sweet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this weirdly touching. As close to a Disney ending as an SCP gets, I think.

However, I have one small question … What happens to the kids?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I knew exactly what it was gonna be when I saw the picture and I saw "divorce lawyer"… and then I didn't. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: No, 18yo me, this is fucking horrifying. Still +1 though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1632-1 refers to a biological entity that consist of

Should that be consists?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. The dialogue felt a bit off at first, but I actually think it ended up helping, in the end. The fact that they are so bizarrely casual about the whole thing, like they are talking to friends at a party instead of a Foundation researcher, really amps up the horror, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. That's chilling.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Other alternative names

"Better mail Dale"

"Better go Joe"

"Better see Lee"

"Better find Arvind"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Better ring Xing."

Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is gonna keep me away from marriage, I swear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slight edit needed.

From the interview log:

From the late nights I spent working, to the heaving drinking later on

While it might've resulted in some serious heaving, I think you meant "heavy" here.

Aside from that, I love it. I love that they're so happy to be together now, because that's creepy as hell. Thanks, Xing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing worse than body horror is making people enjoy it.

Also I would love a description of how the interviewed couple are joined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No worries, I did the same thing once.

Posted a whole damn article and didn't change a single XXXX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Twisted and weird but somehow with a happy ending.

After a few hours, an instance of SCP-1632-1 will appear where the client was.

Was what? Was last seen? Was living? Was located?

Then, I suddenly say Randal next to me.

I believe you mean "saw" next to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dialogue's a little off, but I like it otherwise. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made some minor grammar fixes for you, I hope you don't mind - just things like 'it's -> its' and adding a 'to' to 'all attempts surgical separation'. As for the article itself - not really my sort of thing, but it's a good concept, and well-written enough for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article. A extra-dimensional being saving marriages in its own twisted, anomalous way. The perfect blend of heartwarming and creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Endearing and creepy at the same time. +1. Only caveat would be the dialogue seems a bit stiff, especially because it's trying to convey their rediscovered love for each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the slaphappy fuck did I just read

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems kind of rough, writing-wise. The dialogue in particular feels unrealistic to me at some points. Regardless, the idea is really interesting, and there's nothing extremely wrong with it, so +1. Although I hope that you keep working on this.

EDIT: Well, it's going up, so what the hell do I know? Leave it as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's still a few left, in particular the footnotes.

EDIT: You're good now, take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ how did I forget about that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hella XXXX's bro. Please fix.
